steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: The left side is an independent clause: "Nora sought to increase Latina representation in corporate [BLANK]." The right side is also an independent clause: "[BLANK] founded Las Comadres para las Americas, an international community that for over two decades has served as a resource and information network for Latina business professionals."

steps.title: Apply the independent-clause boundary rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses must be joined by one of: a period (or other terminal punctuation), a semicolon, or a comma plus a FANBOYS coordinating conjunction (and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet). Evaluating each choice:
  • Choice A — ‘settings she’: no punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on (fused) sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘settings, she’: a comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘settings and she’: joining two independent clauses with 'and' requires a comma before the coordinating conjunction; without it, the punctuation is incorrect. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘settings. She’: a period correctly separates the two independent clauses into two sentences. ✓
